speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Michael Jackson. And now we're talking about December 25, 1944, in Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, Flossenburg, was a day-- a day after the spectacle of the hanging that we-- we-- we-- witnessed a day before-- a day before. It wasn't a day of rest and it wasn't a day of holiday. We were-- we were hoping to get out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember-- I remembered talking to a-- to a Polish-Jewish inmate that was in there for a longer time already. And he was so brutal. He told me-- I said, what's going on here? And he grabbed me by the hand, and he took me to the window, and he showed me the crematorium. He said-- And he said to me, here, he said, you will end up in this crematorium. That will be your end. Here, even those that are alive are already dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I was so mad at this guy. I thought to myself, does he-- does he have a sadistic [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] feeling that-- that he knows that we just came in here a day or two before and to try to talk and not-- not-- instead of saying here we are. We-- we knew that the war can't go on forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are in December of 1944. The war ended in four, five weeks-- four, five months, rather, May the 8th. Four months-- a little-- four-- four months and a week, right? Am I right? May the 8th the war ended, and I was liberated on April the 29th.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, this is-- this is what-- when I-- when I think about it today, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I can't-- I can't blame the guy. When you live under-- under a constant dehumanized condition, where-- where death-- death is a relief, if people act the way they act, you-- you-- you can find some kind of a-- some kind of a, not justification, but you can find some understanding why they act the way they do. We stayed in Flossenburg [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGH] about a week or 10 days. No, I don't think it was 10 days. And we were very lucky. Lucky-- we considered ourselves lucky to get out of there. We were loaded up on a train. And we didn't know where we were going. It turned out that they were taking us to Hersbruck. Hersbruck was a subcamp from Flossenburg. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And in-- in Hersbruck, we came in. Again, it took-- it's a very short distance from one place to the other. Hersbruck is very close to-- to Nuremberg. It shouldn't-- it shouldn't have taken more than a couple hours. Why the-- the-- the trains went-- went so-- so long, they went a few miles and then they stopped for hours. I don't know. It was, I think, because the rail lines were-- were interrupted, broken off. They had to wait for-- I don't know. Anyway, when we got into Hersbruck, so it-- I think it was around January the 5th. It might be fifth or sixth. I I am not so sure now, but it couldn't-- it couldn't be-- it couldn't have been any-- any much later. When we got into Hersbruck, and in that camp, we came in, it was very clean. I didn't describe the-- what it looks-- what the-- the beds look. In every camp was the same. Those were bunk beds. They were about this wide, just enough for a person, you know, around 20, 24 inches to-- to-- maybe to turn around. Four, three-- three-- three high, just like chicken coops. But when we came in there, the-- the-- the-- it-- it-- it-- it smelled that it was fresh, clean. It was not-- not like in-- in-- in Flossenburg. The first thing, we-- we found the local-- the-- the-- the ones that were there were not as bitter as-- as the-- the-- the one in-- in-- in Flossenburg. I remember the first ones that greeted us, they told us, that you-- you-- there is a-- they warned us about the kapo of-- of this block. That he is a terrible sadist, to watch out, to watch out not to get in trouble with him. But they were, more or less, friendly. And they warned us do not drink the water, no matter how thirsty you are because there is something in the water that-- that there is dysentery, and people fall like flies after a few-- after a few weeks. They said, if you-- if you feel like you're very thirsty, you-- you should take some snow. You know, we had snow, already, that time, on the ground. And in that-- and in that camp, the next-- the next day, they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] didn't send us out to work. They asked that we should select a-- a-- kapo from among our-- our people. And I remember we had a Hungarian-Jewish boy by the name of [HUNGARIAN] that was very respected from all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And each-- when-- in-- he-- he was, so to speak, regarded as our-- our spokesman, the-- the leader under the Hungarian [INAUDIBLE]. Because even the Hungarians had a little respect for him because of the knowledge of the Hungarian language. He spoke a literary Hungarian language, not a-- not a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how should I say that? Peasant?

speaker: interviewee
text: A peasant language. Or-- or-- no-- it-- it escapes me. I-- I had a very good comparison. Anyway, and he said, children, he said, this is not-- this is not the [NON-ENGLISH]. This is not the labor camp. This is concentration camp. I don't want it. I don't want. And he-- he refused.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, they pulled a guy out, and we later heard some bad things about him. But anyway, that-- that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: The third day, we-- we were assigned to work, as-- to go out to work. Not far from Hersbruck-- I don't know how many kilometers-- there was a village. And in that village, the German Messerschmitt company was building inside a mountain. They were building a-- a air-- air-- aircraft factory, the Messerschmitt aircraft.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the Allies would-- would-- would bombard the-- the-- the factories. But inside the mountain, the-- the-- the-- the-- the tunnel was already dug out deep into the mountain. But they had to-- to carry all kinds of materials into that mountain. And some materials were-- were-- some materials were hoisted by-- what do you call that, [NON-ENGLISH], in German-- a-- a-- wire rail. What do-- you know what I mean? Cables? Cables?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cable. A cable rail. The cable-- Car. Cable car. Cable car rails. They had-- You could-- you could put cement bags or whatnot in there. But long, long logs that are needed. You couldn't put it in-- on a cable. You had to have people carrying that, and carrying it on top of the mountain when it was winter, cold. If we had enough snow, it was fine. You could walk in the snow. But when it froze-- it froze, you-- you slipped on that and then fall. It was terrible. Anyway, we worked on-- we worked on there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 50 days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in 50 days, I-- I dared myself-- I-- the-- one of the-- one of the supervisors was a Yugoslav. And he spoke in Slavic. And-- and he-- he, one time, gave me a piece of bread and he gave me a half a piece of a cigarette. And I thought to myself, oh, he's a nice-- nice guy. And I stupidly allowed myself and I told him-- I told him-- and this must have been, already, January-- January, February-- so I told him you better-- you better treat us nice because the war is going to-- to an end, and-- and Germany is losing the war. And he said, you are so dumb. You will never get out of here alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, yeah. It's not going to last more than about 20, 30 days. He says, you won't last 20, 30 days. After-- after I had this conversation with this guy, I really got-- got scared that I-- I got-- my-- my tongue was loose. How did I dare to talk like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, fortunately, he didn't pursue that. He didn't pursue it. And-- and I got-- after 40 or 50 days-- I won't go into this story here, how I got beaten up. I have no strength to-- to-- to repeat it. I was the one that encouraged many others from our group not to register sick because once you get into the revier, they never see you again. They send you to Flossenburg. And- and you are-- but it came to the point that I had an injury and my-- my foot was frozen and a heavy piece of equipment fell on it in that place. And I couldn't walk anymore. And my feet were swollen. And when I came into the-- to the-- to the camp, I still weighed 80 kilograms. And here, in this-- this cold, I-- [SIGH] I-- I decided I can't carry on anymore. I-- I-- I have to register-- register--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your weight when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember that. The weight, I remember when-- when I was liberated but that's-- I think I shouldn't skip this-- this one story. It's important, for posterity to-- to-- to-- to-- We worked in there. And I'm coming back, because I think it's important. We were suffering so badly from the cold that I thought in order to preserve some heat, body heat-- we didn't have working in that mountain. And by the way, we worked for a German company called Hochtief. That company is still in existence now. It's a construction company. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Cement-- the cement bags was-- were closed up with-- with wires. You know, like-- like you sew-- sew-- sew up at the end with tiny wires. Now, some people, their-- their shoelaces were torn. So they-- they used those wires that were taken off from cement bags to use it, to use it to tie their shoes because you couldn't go out and buy it. Besides, we didn't have for what to buy and what [INAUDIBLE]. Another thing what we did, we took out those empty cement bags. Those were heavy bags that-- that kept 100 pounds or 120 pounds. I don't know how many pounds. And you cut-- we cut out holes on each side. And you cut out the hole for the head here. And the bag was-- so we pull those bags under the shirt in order to-- to preserve some body heat. Because in-- in-- in-- with the-- from the cement bags, at least the-- the-- the wind-- the wind wouldn't-- wouldn't dissipate the body heat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was so lucky that day. Some-- some grandma above in heaven intervened for me. Guys came in, and the kapo noticed that they had these cement bags-- they were empty cement bags that were thrown away-- under there. They beat the guy up so bad that he died next day, for that. I was able to get out from the barrack into the outhouse and get rid of that bag. I don't know how-- how-- who-- who saved me from-- from-- from that terrible crime that I committed. Anyway, it came to the point that I couldn't go on anymore. And I said, I'm going to register sick. When I registered sick, the only thing what they looked, if you had swollen legs, if you were swollen, you know, the legs down here, what were swollen, then they took you in. The-- the doctor took me in, in the-- in the revier in the evening. I-- I had this-- besides that my leg was swollen, this was frozen, and it got swollen. I couldn't even take my shoe off anymore. So he said, yeah, in the morning-- in the morning, you will be operated. He took us in. And I got into the revier. It was very clean in there. The conditions in there were the same bunk beds, the same narrow as before, three high. Because I had injury on my leg, so I got to be on the lowest-- on the lowest one. And the food that was given was given the same in the revier as-- as in the-- in the barracks. The only thing, it was nice and warm inside, and it was clean. And you didn't have to go out to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came into the revier, there was one guy that registered about two, three weeks earlier. I was holding back with all my strength not to go. And when he saw me, he didn't recognize me. He says, oh my god, Michael, what happened to you? What? He still looked half-- half normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him-- I told him what happened. I said, I was afraid to-- to register because they're sending the people-- he said, they are not sending any more because the Russians are already nearby, and they're emptying that camp. They're not sending any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, the next day, there-- there was like a butcher shop. The-- a doctor-- there happened to be a doctor by the name of Dr. Schwartz, that was a major surgeon in Budapest Jewish Hospital. And he was the-- the surgeon but he had nothing, no-- not even an aspirin. And he was-- he was operating on me. He said, it's infected. It has to be opened up. He said, I can't give you-- I have nothing to give you, any anesthesia or anything. It won't hurt you. I'll do it fast. He even asked me, do you speak Hungarian? And-- and two guys were holding me down. It was just on a table and laying down on a table. And he opened it up and cut it open, cut it through and through, drained it out. And-- but he didn't have any gauze. They didn't have any gauze. The only thing they had was-- you know what crepe paper is? You know, those paper-- Crepe paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, that's what he had. And he tied it-- tied it up and put me-- put me in the bed. Well, the untying and the tying-- the untying was worse later on, when he rebandaged this. because there was no gas or an aspirin, nothing he could give me. And I stayed in-- in this hospital. And I have to tell this story. Next to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the-- the-- the bed was two. Like, three high, two wide. Next to me in the bed was a French general. There was a lot of Frenchmen, by the way, in this-- in this camp. And this French general had one foot amputated, and he was supposed to have the other foot amputated, too. We didn't know that that general, that French general, he spoke perfect German. I didn't know-- at that time, I didn't know the German map. I didn't know where-- I knew we were close to Nuremberg, but I didn't know where it-- where it was and how close it is to the relation to the-- to the-- to the war front, the-- the war front. And I remember this general, one-- one day, he talked to me. He said, the war is going to end in a week or two. He said, you will live to see the end of it but I-- I-- I won't. And I have to tell this story about him. This general-- there was a lot of Frenchmen in that revier. They-- they were talking in French. I didn't understand. And I think, by that time, some Frenchmen, French doctors, they were getting some packages through the Red Cross. And they had some-- some medicine. So they were giving him some painkillers. And-- and-- all-- everybody attended to this general.

speaker: interviewer
text: How he got-- how he lost his leg, I didn't-- he didn't-- he didn't tell me. But one day-- one day the-- the head commander of the-- of the camp, in Hersbruck, came in, into the revier. I don't know what rank he had. And he came right over to this French general, and he said to him, [SPEAKING GERMAN] He says, good morning, my colleague. How are you doing? And this French general turns around and in a fine, perfect German, [SPEAKING GERMAN] You-- you dirty pig! I am not your colleague!

speaker: interviewee
text: When I heard this general telling so, I figured he is probably going to shoot him right-- right here. This commander, the German commander from that, he turned right around, and he walked right out. He didn't say a word to this-- to this Frenchman. Anyway, this Frenchman, he lived a few more days, and-- and he-- he passed away in the night. And he was in very big, big pain. He was-- he was crying and-- and-- and he died. But what happened was that the doctor that operated on me, because they knew that this is coming to an end, and he was doing just like in a butcher shop. All he-- all he had is scissors and knives and-- and a table. And he had nothing else that he could give to people. Cutting off limbs and-- and-- and-- and opening up wounds. And somebody-- somebody-- that doctor that was over him, came in just a day before. And they said, he all of a sudden, he died of poison, this Dr. Schwartz. And in the revier, everybody said that he was poisoned. He was poisoned because they didn't want him to live to see, to tell what was going on in this-- in this operating room, this so-called operating room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a few weeks, a few weeks-- it didn't take a few weeks after I was in, we could hear, already, the cannons fire at night. We could hear the front is near. And we got-- we got, one day, the order to-- to evacuate. And of course, because I was-- I was bandaged, I had only one shoe. That shoe, the doctor couldn't take it off. He had to cut the shoe off and throw the shoe away. And-- and that's how-- that's how we were carried onto open wagons, in-- in-- to the-- to the train station and loaded up. OK.
